content.text: vLLM's OpenAI-compatible API server implements the same REST schema as OpenAI's public API — the same endpoints, same request/response JSON shapes, and same SSE streaming format. Any code that uses the OpenAI Python SDK or calls
directly can switch to a self-hosted vLLM server by changing one URL.

attrs.alt: vLLM OpenAI-compatible API request flow: Client → FastAPI Router → AsyncLLMEngine → Response

content.text: The FastAPI layer handles OpenAI-schema validation and translates between the OpenAI request format and vLLM's internal
tracks each request by a UUID so it can abort generation when the client disconnects mid-stream, avoiding wasted GPU compute.

# Minimal start — serves on :8000
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct

# Production start with common options
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --host 0.0.0.0 \
    --port 8000 \
    --tensor-parallel-size 1 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
    --max-model-len 8192 \
    --max-num-seqs 256 \
    --served-model-name llama-3-8b \   # what clients see in GET /v1/models
    --api-key my-secret-key \           # enforce Bearer token auth
    --enable-prefix-caching \
    --uvicorn-log-level warning
flag lets you present an alias (e.g.
) so clients that hard-code a model name in their SDK call don't need changes. The real model loaded is whatever you pass to

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="my-secret-key",
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3-8b",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a concise assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is the KV-cache?"},
    ],
    temperature=0.7,
    max_tokens=256,
    top_p=0.95,
)

# Identical structure to OpenAI response
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
print(f"Usage: {response.usage.prompt_tokens} in, {response.usage.completion_tokens} out")

stream = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3-8b",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Count slowly to 10"}],
    max_tokens=128,
    stream=True,
)

collected = []
for chunk in stream:
    delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content
    if delta:
        collected.append(delta)
        print(delta, end="", flush=True)
print()
full_text = "".join(collected)
constrains generation to valid JSON using guided decoding:

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3-8b",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Return JSON only."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Give me a user object with name, age, email fields."},
    ],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
    max_tokens=128,
    temperature=0.1,
)

import json
data = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
print(data)   # {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 28, 'email': 'alice@example.com'}
extension parameter (in the extra_body) that accepts a full JSON Schema, constraining tokens at the logit level so the output is guaranteed to validate:

USER_SCHEMA = {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
        "name": {"type": "string"},
        "age": {"type": "integer", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 150},
        "email": {"type": "string", "format": "email"},
    },
    "required": ["name", "age", "email"],
}

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3-8b",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Generate a sample user"}],
    max_tokens=128,
    extra_body={"guided_json": USER_SCHEMA},
)

import json
user = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
# Guaranteed to match USER_SCHEMA — age is always an int, required fields present
in the OpenAI SDK. These expose vLLM-specific features not in the OpenAI spec:
Constrain output to match the schema (guaranteed valid JSON)
Constrain output to match a regex pattern
Constrain output to one of the listed strings
Constrain output to a context-free grammar (EBNF)
Whether to include BOS/EOS tokens in output (default True)
Include the stop string in the returned text
Return top-N token log probabilities per position
Return log probs for the prompt tokens too

# Constrain output to a regex (e.g. ISO date format)
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3-8b",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What date is Christmas 2025?"}],
    max_tokens=16,
    extra_body={"guided_regex": r"\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}"},
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)   # "2025-12-25"

# Get top-5 log probs per output token
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3-8b",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "The capital of France is"}],
    max_tokens=5,
    logprobs=True,
    top_logprobs=5,
)
for token_info in response.choices[0].logprobs.content:
    print(token_info.token, round(token_info.logprob, 3))
header on every request. For multi-tenant setups, run separate vLLM instances per tenant or place an API gateway (e.g. Kong, Traefik) in front that validates JWTs and proxies to the shared vLLM:

# Client side — identical to calling OpenAI with an API key
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="my-secret-key",     # sent as Authorization: Bearer my-secret-key
)

# Verify the server is live before sending requests
import httpx

def wait_for_vllm(url: str, timeout: float = 60) -> None:
    """Poll /health until the server is ready."""
    import time
    deadline = time.time() + timeout
    while time.time() < deadline:
        try:
            r = httpx.get(f"{url}/health", timeout=2)
            if r.status_code == 200:
                return
        except httpx.ConnectError:
            pass
        time.sleep(1)
    raise TimeoutError(f"vLLM at {url} did not become healthy in {timeout}s")

wait_for_vllm("http://localhost:8000")
The migration is a three-line change for most codebases:

# Before (calling OpenAI directly)
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()   # uses OPENAI_API_KEY env var, base_url=https://api.openai.com

# After (calling vLLM)
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://your-vllm-host:8000/v1",
    api_key="not-checked-by-default",   # or your --api-key value
)
# model name must match --served-model-name on the vLLM server

# Everything else is identical:
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3-8b",   # was "gpt-4o"
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}],
)
Parameters that exist in the OpenAI API but are silently ignored by vLLM (because they are OpenAI-specific):
SamplingParam). Parameters that do NOT exist in OpenAI but work in vLLM: all

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    model="llama-3-8b",
    openai_api_base="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    openai_api_key="not-required",
    temperature=0.7,
    max_tokens=512,
)

from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
response = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content="Explain attention in one paragraph")])
print(response.content)
The recommended way to run vLLM in production is a Docker container with the official image, which bundles the right CUDA and cuDNN versions:

# Pull the official vLLM image (replace TAG with the version you want)
docker pull vllm/vllm-openai:latest

# Run with GPU access, model from HuggingFace Hub
docker run --gpus all \
    -p 8000:8000 \
    -e HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN=$HF_TOKEN \
    --shm-size=16g \                  # shared memory for tensor-parallel inter-process
    vllm/vllm-openai:latest \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --tensor-parallel-size 1 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
    --served-model-name llama-3-8b \
    --api-key $VLLM_API_KEY

# Kubernetes Deployment (single-GPU)
# Add resource limit: nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
# Liveness probe: GET /health (200 OK)
# Readiness probe: GET /health (200 OK, delay 60s)

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: vllm-server
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: vllm
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: vllm
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: vllm
        image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest
        args:
        - --model
        - meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct
        - --served-model-name
        - llama-3-8b
        - --gpu-memory-utilization
        - "0.90"
        - --max-num-seqs
        - "256"
        env:
        - name: HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: hf-token
              key: token
        resources:
          limits:
            nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
            memory: "24Gi"
          requests:
            nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 60
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 60
          periodSeconds: 5
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000

vLLM itself does not implement rate limiting — that belongs in a gateway or reverse proxy in front of it. For multi-replica deployments, a load balancer that distributes at the request level (not connection level) is important because LLM requests vary widely in duration:
Request-Level Rate Limiting with a Gateway

# Simple token-bucket rate limiter as FastAPI middleware
# Deploy in front of vLLM or as a sidecar proxy
import time
from collections import defaultdict
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException
import httpx

app = FastAPI()

RATE_LIMIT = 60        # requests per minute per API key
WINDOW = 60            # seconds
VLLM_BASE = "http://localhost:8000"

_counters: dict[str, list[float]] = defaultdict(list)

def check_rate_limit(api_key: str) -> None:
    now = time.time()
    window_start = now - WINDOW
    # Purge old timestamps
    _counters[api_key] = [t for t in _counters[api_key] if t > window_start]
    if len(_counters[api_key]) >= RATE_LIMIT:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=429,
            detail=f"Rate limit {RATE_LIMIT} req/min exceeded",
            headers={"Retry-After": "60"},
        )
    _counters[api_key].append(now)

@app.api_route("/v1/{path:path}", methods=["GET", "POST"])
async def proxy(path: str, request: Request):
    api_key = request.headers.get("Authorization", "anon").replace("Bearer ", "")
    check_rate_limit(api_key)

    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=120) as client:
        resp = await client.request(
            method=request.method,
            url=f"{VLLM_BASE}/v1/{path}",
            headers=dict(request.headers),
            content=await request.body(),
        )
    return resp
Quick smoke-tests you can run from the terminal to verify each endpoint:

BASE="http://localhost:8000"
KEY="my-secret-key"

# 1. Health check
curl -s $BASE/health

# 2. List models
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" $BASE/v1/models | python3 -m json.tool

# 3. Chat completion
curl -s -X POST $BASE/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model":"llama-3-8b","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hi"}],"max_tokens":32}' \
    | python3 -m json.tool

# 4. Streaming
curl -s -X POST $BASE/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model":"llama-3-8b","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Count to 3"}],"max_tokens":32,"stream":true}'

# 5. Tokenize (vLLM extension)
curl -s -X POST $BASE/v1/tokenize \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model":"llama-3-8b","prompt":"Hello world"}'
The vLLM OpenAI-compatible API is not 100% identical. Knowing the gaps prevents surprises:
Supported for models with tool-call templates (Llama-3, Mistral, etc.)
Supported for vision models (LLaVA, Phi-3-Vision) when loaded
Not implemented — use separate training pipeline
Use vLLM offline LLM class directly for batching
vLLM's OpenAI-compatible API server makes self-hosted LLM serving a drop-in replacement for the OpenAI API. Start the server with
, point any OpenAI SDK client at your host:port with the matching model name, and the migration is complete. Use
to present an alias matching existing client code,
for structured outputs that the base OpenAI spec does not guarantee. The
endpoints integrate directly with Kubernetes liveness probes and Prometheus.
